title: Test suite is structurally blind to the known incident classes: mocks pin the exact dead seams (FutureApp current_app, oauth_tokens, FAILED-vs-CANCELLED, stale-cursor selection, RUNNING false-failure), affirms the pg_catalog disclosure as contract, and tests no role enforcement
claim: A systematic mock audit shows the suite cannot detect the known production bugs because tests mock or pin the exact failing seams: (1) reconcile_stale_thread_job's RUNNING false-failure branch is never constructed — every stale-reconcile test builds PENDING via _make_stale_pending_job and _procrastinate_job_status is an AsyncMock, so the known false-failure ships unguarded. (2) jobs_cancel tests patch apps.workspaces.api.jobs_cancel.current_app, the same module-level FutureApp import-time binding that is a known surviving sibling site, so the binding failure can't fire and cancellation-reaches-Procrastinate is unverified. (3) MCP tool tests set mock_run.tenant_schema.tenant_membership.tenant.external_id, a path that doesn't exist on the real model (production reads run.tenant_schema.tenant.external_id), passing only because MagicMock fabricates attributes — proving contract-drift blindness. (4) Resume test asserts 'oauth_tokens' in agent config, codifying the dead OAuth plumbing as contract. (5) sql_validator's TestPromptValidatorAlignment pins unqualified pg_catalog views and public.* as ALLOWED, certifying the known cross-tenant disclosure vector. (6) cancel_materialization test asserts only data.cancelled True, never the written state, so it passes before/after a FAILED-vs-CANCELLED fix. (7) stale-cursor regression injects prior_run via the mocked filter/exclude/order_by chain, so the real prior-run selection query is untested and the two-registries finding is uncatchable. (8) merge-users suite has zero TenantMetadata assertions, so the cascade-delete data-loss path is invisible. (9) OCS loader tests are happy-path MagicMock only (no 401/403/malformed/retry), mirroring the Connect-only hardening asymmetry at the test layer. (10) transformation trigger test mocks run_transformation_pipeline to return instantly, hiding synchronous in-request dbt execution and the dbt-superuser class.
chain: Per-bug seam mocking: _make_stale_pending_job only builds PENDING; tests patch jobs_cancel.current_app; tests use nonexistent tenant_membership.tenant.external_id; resume test asserts oauth_tokens; sql_validator pins pg_catalog/public allowed; cancel test asserts only data.cancelled; stale-cursor test injects prior_run via mocked filter chain; merge suite no TenantMetadata; OCS tests happy-path only; trigger test mocks pipeline instantly

votes.evidence:
The finding is a 10-part meta-claim. 9 sub-claims are factually accurate test-code descriptions, but the HEADLINE claim (#1, also in the title) is materially FALSE, and the finding's file list omits the test file that refutes it.

REFUTED — Sub-claim #1: Finding says "reconcile_stale_thread_job's RUNNING false-failure branch is never constructed — every stale-reconcile test builds PENDING via _make_stale_pending_job and _procrastinate_job_status is an AsyncMock, so the known false-failure ships unguarded." This is false. tests/test_threadjob_janitor.py (NOT in the finding's files list) contains: test_janitor_marks_stuck_running_failed_without_deferring_resume (line 181: `state=ThreadJob.State.RUNNING`) and test_janitor_persists_synthetic_message_on_stuck_running (line 232: RUNNING) — both exercise the RUNNING branch of reconcile_stale_thread_job (tasks.py:750-775). Furthermore test_procrastinate_job_status_reads_status_from_orm_row (line 354) and test_janitor_reconciles_stale_job_against_real_failed_procrastinate_row (line 376, docstring: "no mock of _procrastinate_job_status") insert REAL rows into procrastinate_jobs and call the real status function — contradicting "_procrastinate_job_status is an AsyncMock."

CONFIRMED — #2: jobs_cancel.py:11 `from procrastinate.contrib.django.procrastinate_app import current_app`; tests patch it at test_jobs_endpoints.py:393,484. Severity overstated: jobs_cancel.py:47 wraps the abort in try/except and the DB flip IS verified (test lines 400-402).
CONFIRMED — #3: test_mcp_tenant_tools.py:759,803 set `mock_run.tenant_schema.tenant_membership.tenant.external_id` but server.py:426,484 read `run.tenant_schema.tenant.external_id`; models.py:28 shows TenantSchema.tenant is a FK with no tenant_membership attr. MagicMock fabricates it; test never asserts tenant_id.
CONFIRMED (nuance) — #4: test_resume_thread_task.py:121 asserts `"oauth_tokens" in config`. oauth_tokens IS dead: base.py build_agent_graph (line 485) accepts the param but never references it in its body (grep of body NR>495 finds nothing); no node/tool reads config["oauth_tokens"]. Same dead plumbing also lives in the LIVE chat path (chat/views.py:196), not only resume.
CONFIRMED — #5: test_sql_validator.py:514 test_unqualified_pg_catalog_views_are_allowed and lines 181-182/201-203 pin public.* and pg_catalog views as ALLOWED.
CONFIRMED — #6: test_mcp_tenant_tools.py test_cancel_in_progress_run (line 818-820) asserts only data.cancelled and run_id, never the written run.state.
CONFIRMED — #7: test_materializer.py:870 injects prior_run as a MagicMock via _run_connect_pipeline(prior_run=prior), bypassing the real prior-run selection query.
CONFIRMED — #8: zero TenantMetadata references in test_merge_users_service.py; models.py:266-268 TenantMetadata.tenant_membership is OneToOneField(on_delete=CASCADE); merge.py:166,298 delete conflicting duplicate TenantMembership rows → plausible untested cascade data-loss.
CONFIRMED — #9: every OCS loader test uses status_code=200 MagicMock; no 401/403/malformed/retry.
CONFIRMED — #10: transformations/views.py:122-166 trigger action calls run_transformation_pipeline synchronously in-request (docstring "synchronously"); test_transformation_api.py:399-402 mocks it to return instantly. Endpoint live at /api/transformations/runs/trigger/.

Reachability of all affected paths confirmed: janitor is @app.periodic cron (tasks.py:819), cancel endpoints routed, MCP tools @mcp.tool(), resume a procrastinate task, trigger a DRF @action, merge a management command.
votes.notes: Verdict is PARTIAL, not REFUTED, because the finding's core thesis (the suite has systematic mock-based blind spots) is supported by 9 of 10 accurate sub-observations. But it is not CONFIRMED because: (a) the headline/title claim and sub-claim #1 are materially false — the RUNNING false-failure branch and the real (unmocked) _procrastinate_job_status path ARE covered in test_threadjob_janitor.py, a file the finding does not even cite; (b) severity is inflated by the word "unguarded/ships" — most confirmed items are test-quality nits (contract-drift, missing negative assertions) rather than production defects shipping uncaught, and the highest-stakes user-visible behaviors (cancel DB flip, RUNNING reconcile, cancel-state preservation in the pipeline) ARE tested elsewhere. The DEBT status and velocity impact are appropriate for the genuine portion. Recommend the finding be rewritten to drop sub-claim #1 and to cite test_threadjob_janitor.py as the counter-evidence.

title: test_workspace_permissions.py has zero role assertions and dead role fixtures; permissions.py has zero importers anywhere
claim: Adjudication of the dead-code question, strengthened: IsWorkspaceMember/IsWorkspaceReadWrite/IsWorkspaceManager in apps/workspaces/permissions.py are not 'imported but ineffective' — grep across apps/ and tests/ finds zero importers; they are entirely unreferenced. The suite named test_workspace_permissions.py defines read_client/write_client fixtures (lines 18-27) used by no test, and every test asserts member-vs-non-member only. Role enforcement exists and is tested solely on transformations (test_transformation_api.py:152,236,311,454) and refresh (test_refresh_endpoint.py:61); jobs/knowledge/recipes/artifacts/chat suites use READ_WRITE fixtures exclusively, making the known WorkspaceRole-unenforced gap structurally invisible to the test suite.
chain: grep finds zero importers of permissions.py classes; test_workspace_permissions.py:18-27 read/write fixtures unused, tests assert member-vs-non-member only; role enforcement tested only on transformations+refresh

votes.evidence:
CONFIRMED SUB-CLAIMS (re-traced independently):

1. Zero importers of permissions.py classes. `apps/workspaces/permissions.py:21,28,36` define IsWorkspaceMember/IsWorkspaceReadWrite/IsWorkspaceManager. Repo-wide grep for these class names and for any `permissions` import path returns only `rest_framework.permissions` imports (e.g. `apps/transformations/views.py:5 "from rest_framework.permissions import IsAuthenticated"`). No view, test, or other module references the three classes. Entirely dead code — not wired to any route.

2. Dead fixtures in tests/test_workspace_permissions.py. `read_client` (lines 18-21) and `write_client` (lines 24-27) are defined and used by zero tests in the file. All 8 tests use `auth_client`/`client`+`other_user` and assert only 200-for-member vs 403-for-non-member (e.g. lines 61-64, 95-98). No role-differentiated assertion exists in the file.

3. Role gap is real and structurally invisible in content apps. `apps/knowledge/api/views.py`, `apps/recipes/api/views.py`, `apps/artifacts/`, `apps/chat/` contain zero `WorkspaceRole`/`.role` checks (grep over those apps matches only an artifacts test file). Meanwhile test_jobs_endpoints.py and test_chat_thread_ownership.py create READ_WRITE memberships exclusively (e.g. test_jobs_endpoints.py:33, test_chat_thread_ownership.py:49), and knowledge/recipes/artifacts suites use the conftest `user` fixture which holds MANAGE (tests/conftest.py:71). No suite for those apps ever exercises a READ-role user against a write endpoint, so the missing enforcement cannot be caught.

4. Role tests on transformations and refresh exist as cited: tests/test_transformation_api.py:152 (`test_create_workspace_asset_read_role_forbidden`), :236, :311, :454 (read_user 403s); tests/test_refresh_endpoint.py:61 (`test_read_only_user_cannot_trigger_refresh`); enforcement is inline in views (apps/transformations/views.py:84-87,154; apps/workspaces/api/views.py:330-332), not via the dead permission classes.

REFUTED SUB-CLAIM: "Role enforcement exists and is tested SOLELY on transformations and refresh" is false. tests/test_workspace_management.py also asserts role enforcement with inline role fixtures: `test_non_manager_cannot_rename` (lines 136-147, READ_WRITE user gets 403 on PATCH rename), `test_non_manager_cannot_delete` (~192-200, READ user 403 on DELETE), `test_read_write_member_cannot_remove_others` (~255-267), and a READ_WRITE-cannot-add-members test (~355-369, "Only managers can add members."). Workspace-core role enforcement (rename/delete/member management) is tested.

ADDITIONAL UNTESTED ROLE CHECK FOUND: apps/workspaces/api/views.py:500-503 forbids READ-role table annotation; no test exercises that 403 branch (no annotation role test found in tests/).
votes.notes: Verdict is PARTIAL only because of the "solely transformations+refresh" overstatement and the imprecise "READ_WRITE fixtures exclusively" detail (knowledge/recipes/artifacts actually run as MANAGE via the conftest `user` fixture; jobs/chat are the READ_WRITE-only ones). The headline claims — permissions.py fully dead with zero importers, test_workspace_permissions.py has dead role fixtures and zero role assertions, and the READ-role enforcement gap in knowledge/recipes/artifacts/chat is invisible to the suite — are all CONFIRMED. Status DEBT and impact security are correct: the dead classes are unreachable (no route uses them), but the masked gap means READ-role members can perform writes in four apps today with no failing test. Suggested fix scope: either wire the DRF permission classes into the nested workspace routes or delete permissions.py, and add READ-role 403 tests for knowledge/recipes/artifacts/chat write endpoints plus the annotation PUT at apps/workspaces/api/views.py:500.

votes.evidence:
CONFIRMED ELEMENTS (independently re-traced):

1. Zero importers of permissions.py classes. Ran `grep -rn "IsWorkspaceMember|IsWorkspaceReadWrite|IsWorkspaceManager|workspaces.permissions"` across the whole repo: only matches are the definition file itself, /Users/bderenzi/Code/dimagi/scout/apps/workspaces/permissions.py:21,28,36 (plus an identical stale copy under .claude/worktrees/integration-incident-fixes/). No view, urlconf, or test references them. Reachability: NOT wired to any live route — entirely dead code. Actual enforcement is inline: apps/workspaces/api/workspace_views.py:28,302,333,390,464,503,554,600 (`if membership.role != WorkspaceRole.MANAGE`), apps/workspaces/api/views.py:330 (`membership.role not in (READ_WRITE, MANAGE)` for refresh) and views.py:500 (`if membership.role == WorkspaceRole.READ` for table-annotation PUT).

2. Dead fixtures + member-only assertions in tests/test_workspace_permissions.py. Lines 18-27 define `read_client`/`write_client`; none of the file's 8 tests use them (all use `auth_client` or `client`+`other_user`), and every assertion is member 200 vs non-member 403 — zero role-differentiated assertions. Fixtures are file-local so they cannot be used elsewhere.

3. read_user-driven role-403 tests exist where claimed: tests/test_transformation_api.py:152 (`test_create_workspace_asset_read_role_forbidden`), ~236 (update), ~311 (delete), ~454 (`test_trigger_workspace_read_role_forbidden`); tests/test_refresh_endpoint.py:61 (`test_read_only_user_cannot_trigger_refresh`). Repo-wide, the `read_user` fixture (tests/conftest.py:76) is consumed only by these two suites.

4. jobs/knowledge/recipes/artifacts/chat suites contain no role-enforcement assertions. Caveat to "READ_WRITE exclusively": tests/test_jobs_endpoints.py:420 does create a WorkspaceRole.READ membership, but only for an ownership test (`test_cancel_job_cross_user_blocked` asserts 404 cross-user; comment at :411 says membership "lets them through") — not a role assertion. test_artifacts.py:314 uses MANAGE. Substance holds: no suite asserts READ-role write restriction on content endpoints.

REFUTED ELEMENT:

5. "Role enforcement exists and is tested solely on transformations and refresh" is FALSE. tests/test_workspace_management.py tests MANAGE-role enforcement with role-403 assertions: test_non_manager_cannot_rename:136-147 (READ_WRITE user PATCH → 403), test_non_manager_cannot_delete:193-200, test_read_write_member_cannot_remove_others:256-267, test_non_manager_cannot_add_members:353-368. Additionally, views.py:500 (table-annotation READ-role block) is a third enforcement site, apparently untested (the only "role required" grep hit in tests/test_mcp_tenant_tools.py:904 is an unrelated comment about a Postgres SET ROLE).
votes.notes: Verdict is PARTIAL because the central facts are real and reproduced (permissions.py is fully dead code with zero importers; test_workspace_permissions.py has dead read/write fixtures and only member-vs-non-member assertions; content suites never assert READ-role restrictions), but the mechanism summary overstates the test gap: workspace-management endpoints (rename/delete/member CRUD in workspace_views.py) enforce MANAGE inline and ARE tested with role-403 assertions in test_workspace_management.py, so role enforcement is tested on three surfaces (transformations, refresh, workspace management), not two. The genuinely untested role surfaces are: table annotation PUT (views.py:500, enforced but untested) and READ-role write restriction on knowledge/recipes/artifacts/chat/jobs content endpoints (no enforcement test anywhere — and whether enforcement even exists there was not established by this finding). Status DEBT and impact security are appropriate: dead permission classes plus the content-endpoint test blind spot keep any READ-role write gap invisible to CI.

title: CI sets neither DATABASE_URL nor MANAGED_DATABASE_URL, so every real-DB incident-regression suite is skipped under a green badge
claim: Extends the known CI-skip finding: .github/workflows/ci.yml test-job env defines DATABASE_USER/PASSWORD/TEST_DATABASE_NAME/HOST/PORT but not DATABASE_URL, so even suites with a DATABASE_URL fallback skip. CI-dead set: test_view_schema_builder.py (788-LOC truncation-incident regressions), test_ocs_materializer.py, TestConnectPageReplayIdempotency (the f26c1a0/2587158 dedup pin with realistic id-less payloads — it exists and is well-built but never executes in CI), TestWriteCases/TestWriteForms, and TestExecuteAsyncIntegration (SET ROLE/search_path/statement_timeout). A green CI badge certifies none of the 'is the incident actually fixed' tests.
chain: ci.yml env block (no DATABASE_URL/MANAGED_DATABASE_URL) -> module/class skipif guards in listed suites evaluate true -> pytest skips -> CI green without executing any incident regression

votes.evidence:
Independently re-traced the full chain; every link reproduced.

1) CI env block: .github/workflows/ci.yml lines 54-61 set DATABASE_USER/DATABASE_PASSWORD/TEST_DATABASE_NAME/DATABASE_HOST/DATABASE_PORT plus DJANGO_SECRET_KEY/DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE. grep for "DATABASE_URL|MANAGED_DATABASE_URL" in ci.yml returns NEITHER FOUND. Test step is just `uv run pytest`.

2) No env injection compensates. pyproject.toml [tool.pytest.ini_options] has no pytest-env plugin and no env section (addopts only `-v --tb=short -m 'not smoke'`). tests/conftest.py and config/settings/test.py never set os.environ["DATABASE_URL"]/["MANAGED_DATABASE_URL"]. test.py line 16 comment even states: "CI: sets DATABASE_USER/PASSWORD/HOST/PORT explicitly (no DATABASE_URL)."

3) Skip guards read os.environ directly, so they evaluate true in CI:
- tests/test_view_schema_builder.py:19-22 module-level `pytestmark = pytest.mark.skipif(not os.environ.get("MANAGED_DATABASE_URL"), ...)`. 788 LOC, 13 test fns.
- tests/test_ocs_materializer.py:22-24 same module-level skipif on MANAGED_DATABASE_URL. 5 test fns.
- tests/test_materializer.py: TestWriteCases (class line 1079, guard 1090-1092), TestWriteForms (1133, guard 1141-1143), TestConnectPageReplayIdempotency (1172, _get_db_url at 1187-1190 returns `MANAGED_DATABASE_URL or DATABASE_URL`, per-test skips at 1209-1211/1290-1292/1359-1361). Because CI has neither var, the `or DATABASE_URL` fallback also yields None -> pytest.skip. 8 test fns in this region.
- tests/test_mcp_tenant_tools.py:853 TestExecuteAsyncIntegration, real_db fixture at 864-872 does `db_url = os.environ.get("DATABASE_URL"); if not db_url: pytest.skip("No DATABASE_URL for integration test")`. 5 test fns; fixture comment references SET ROLE read-only-role setup.

4) Reachability of the guarded production code (all live today):
- build_view_schema is invoked from the post-materialization Procrastinate task at apps/workspaces/tasks.py:324 (`await asyncio.to_thread(SchemaManager().build_view_schema, workspace)`).
- _write_cases/_write_forms/_write_connect_payments are dispatched via the loader registry in mcp_server/services/materializer.py:762-795 (defs at 1112/1179/1665).
- _execute_async with `SET ROLE`/`SET statement_timeout`/RESET ROLE lives at mcp_server/services/query.py:38-65 (the live read-only query path).

5) addopts `-m 'not smoke'` is irrelevant: these suites are not smoke-marked; they skip solely via the env-var guards. Net: a green CI run executes none of the listed incident-regression tests (~31 test fns across the 5 named suites), yet the badge is green.
votes.notes: Status DEBT and impact velocity are accurate. The finding's most contestable claim — that TestConnectPageReplayIdempotency's `MANAGED_DATABASE_URL or DATABASE_URL` fallback would still run in CI — is correctly handled: CI provides NEITHER variable, so the fallback resolves to None and the test skips. No env injection anywhere (pyproject/conftest/test.py) rescues it. Minor: finding cites test_materializer.py:1173 for the dedup class; the class/decorator is at 1172-1173 (within tolerance). I did not literally run `uv run pytest` in CI conditions (would require unsetting local env), but the guards read os.environ and CI demonstrably sets neither var, so the skip is deterministic. Confidence high.

title: OCS participants sync is team-wide, not chatbot-scoped: Scout sends a 'chatbot' param upstream silently ignores, loading whole-team rosters + per-chatbot custom data into a single-chatbot tenant schema
claim: Scout's OCSParticipantLoader passes params={'chatbot': experiment_id}, matching the OCS OpenAPI docs. But the upstream view's get() body (verified verbatim from dimagi/open-chat-studio@main apps/api/views/participants.py) reads only 'identifier', 'platform', and 'experiment' from query_params — the documented 'chatbot' param is unimplemented and DRF ignores unknown params. The filter therefore never applies: every OCS participants materialization loads ALL participants of the whole OCS team, each with their per-chatbot custom 'data' arrays for EVERY chatbot in the team (unfiltered data_qs prefetched and serialized), and writes them into a tenant schema whose boundary is one chatbot. Cross-chatbot participant rosters and custom data (PII-class payloads) become queryable by the agent and workspace members granted only this chatbot. Invisible on single-chatbot teams. Scout-side fix is one token: send 'experiment' instead of 'chatbot'.
chain: pipelines/ocs_sync.yml source 'participants' -> ocs_participants.py:44-47 params={'chatbot': experiment_id} -> upstream participants.py get() reads only 'experiment' -> unfiltered qs=Participant.objects.filter(team=request.team) with all-chatbot data_qs prefetch -> materializer ~L1379 ON CONFLICT (participant_id) writes whole team into raw_participants

votes.evidence: Scout side: mcp_server/loaders/ocs_participants.py:44-47 — `url = f"{self.base_url}/api/participants"`; `params = {"chatbot": self.experiment_id}` passed to self._paginate (ocs_base.py:54-85, which forwards params verbatim to requests). Contrast: mcp_server/loaders/ocs_sessions.py:25 uses `params = {"experiment": self.experiment_id}` — the participants loader is the odd one out. Upstream (independently fetched dimagi/open-chat-studio origin/main @ 0d41a8573, 2026-06-12): apps/api/views/participants.py ParticipantView.get() reads only `request.query_params.get("identifier")`, `.get("platform")`, and `_parse_experiment_uuid(request.query_params.get("experiment"))`; the OpenApiParameter named "chatbot" in the @extend_schema block has no corresponding code — `chatbot=` is silently ignored by DRF. `_parse_experiment_uuid(None)` returns None, so `qs = Participant.objects.filter(team=request.team)` stays team-wide and `data_qs = ParticipantData.objects.select_related("experiment").filter(team=request.team)` (all chatbots) is prefetched into each participant's `data` array. Reachability: pipelines/ocs_sync.yml:16-17 declares the `participants` source; mcp_server/services/materializer.py:830 maps it to (OCSParticipantLoader, _write_ocs_participants); _load_ocs_source (817, experiment_id = tenant_membership.tenant.external_id at 825) dispatched via _load_source:754-757 for provider=="ocs", called from the materialize loop at line 702; entry point is the live procrastinate task materialize_workspace (apps/workspaces/tasks.py:204). Writer _write_ocs_participants (materializer.py:1007-1057) DROP/CREATEs {schema}.raw_participants and inserts every fetched row including full `data` JSONB (_OCS_PARTICIPANTS_INSERT at 1374-1384, ON CONFLICT (participant_id) DO UPDATE). Net effect reproduced: a single-chatbot tenant schema receives the whole OCS team's participant roster plus per-chatbot custom data for every chatbot in the team, queryable by the agent and workspace members.
votes.notes: Verified upstream against origin/main fetched today (0d41a85, 2026-06-12) from the local /Users/bderenzi/Code/dimagi/open-chat-studio clone — not the finding's quotes. Minor corrections/additions: (1) the materializer line for the upsert is 1374-1384, not ~1379, and the DROP/CREATE in _write_ocs_participants means each run fully rewrites the table — the ON CONFLICT clause is belt-and-suspenders, not the leak mechanism; (2) the proposed one-token fix (send "experiment") is correct against current upstream behavior, and with it applied the upstream filter also scopes the data array (data_qs filtered by experiment__public_id) — but this couples Scout to an undocumented param name; the upstream doc/code mismatch (documented "chatbot" unimplemented) should be reported/fixed in open-chat-studio as well, ideally accepting both names; (3) scope of the leak is intra-OCS-team — the API credential already has team-wide read at OCS (1 credential = 1 team), so the violated boundary is Scout's chatbot-scoped tenant model (workspace members without the OCS credential see other chatbots' rosters/PII), which still justifies security/BROKEN-NOW.

votes.evidence:
Scout side (re-read from working tree):
- /Users/bderenzi/Code/dimagi/scout/mcp_server/loaders/ocs_participants.py:44-47 — `url = f"{self.base_url}/api/participants"` and `params = {"chatbot": self.experiment_id}`, with the docstring (lines 26-27) asserting this scopes the list to the tenant's chatbot. `_map_participant` (line 74) copies the full per-chatbot `data` array verbatim: `"data": raw.get("data") or []`.
- /Users/bderenzi/Code/dimagi/scout/mcp_server/loaders/ocs_base.py:54-86 — `_paginate` passes params on the first request only; no other filtering.

Upstream side (fetched fresh from dimagi/open-chat-studio@main, apps/api/views/participants.py, not trusting the finding's quotes):
- The `get()` body reads exactly three params: `request.query_params.get("identifier")`, `.get("platform")`, and `_parse_experiment_uuid(request.query_params.get("experiment"))`. The string "chatbot" appears only inside the `@extend_schema` OpenApiParameter docs. DRF silently ignores unknown query params, so `?chatbot=<uuid>` is a no-op.
- With no `experiment` param: `data_qs = ParticipantData.objects.select_related("experiment").filter(team=request.team)` (all chatbots) and `qs = Participant.objects.filter(team=request.team)` (whole team), with `Prefetch("data_set", queryset=data_qs, ...)` — so every team participant is returned with custom data entries for every chatbot in the team.
- Root cause located: upstream commit f1cad548 (2026-05-13, "Rename experiment query param to chatbot") changed ONLY the OpenApiParameter name in the docs decorator; the diff shows zero changes to the get() body, which still reads "experiment". I verified the commit diff via the GitHub API.

Reachability (live path):
- /Users/bderenzi/Code/dimagi/scout/pipelines/ocs_sync.yml:16-17 declares the `participants` source; pipeline_registry.get_by_provider("ocs") loads it.
- /Users/bderenzi/Code/dimagi/scout/apps/workspaces/tasks.py:173 and :496 — the procrastinate `materialize_workspace` task calls `run_pipeline`, which iterates `pipeline.sources` (materializer.py:259, :702) → `_load_source` (:754-755, provider == "ocs") → `_load_ocs_source` (:817-838) → `loader_map["participants"] = (OCSParticipantLoader, _write_ocs_participants)` (:830).
- /Users/bderenzi/Code/dimagi/scout/mcp_server/services/materializer.py:1374-1383 — `_OCS_PARTICIPANTS_INSERT` writes `(participant_id, identifier, name, platform, remote_id, data)` into `{schema}.raw_participants` with `ON CONFLICT (participant_id) DO UPDATE`, so the whole-team roster plus all-chatbot custom data lands in the single-chatbot tenant schema, queryable by the agent and workspace members.
- OCS tenants are live (TenantConnection feature, PR #220 merged; credential model is 1 credential = 1 team), so multi-chatbot teams hit this on every materialization.

Fix verification: passing `experiment=<uuid>` against current upstream main filters both `qs` and `data_qs` (the `if experiment_uuid := ...` branch), so the one-token Scout-side fix works today.
votes.notes: Fully reproduced, including the upstream mechanism. Additional finding beyond the report: the docs/implementation divergence was introduced by upstream commit f1cad548 (2026-05-13), which renamed the param in the OpenAPI decorator only — Scout's loader (written against OCS PR #3334's docs) faithfully followed broken documentation. Two caveats on the proposed fix: (1) I verified upstream @main, not the code actually deployed at www.openchatstudio.com, though the mismatch has existed since 2026-05-13 so any recent deploy has it; (2) since upstream clearly intends to rename "experiment" -> "chatbot", a later upstream fix could rename the body param and break a Scout that sends only "experiment" — sending BOTH params ({"experiment": id, "chatbot": id}) is the robust Scout-side fix, ideally paired with an upstream issue/PR to make the view honor the documented "chatbot" param. Also worth a defensive check: Scout could post-filter rows whose data entries reference other chatbot_ids, or assert that returned data arrays only contain the expected chatbot_id, to fail loudly if the upstream contract drifts again.

title: CommCare HQ actively rate-limits both APIs (429 + Retry-After by design); Scout's CommCare loaders have no retry and fail the run — raises the known Connect-only-hardening finding to expected-in-production for CommCare
claim: Verified upstream: case_api (Case API v2) is decorated @api_throttle which returns HTTP 429 with a Retry-After header (corehq/apps/api/decorators.py:51-61), and the tastypie Forms v0.5 resource uses HQThrottle via CustomResourceMeta. Limits include a constant floor of per_second=1, per_minute=10, per_hour=30, per_day=50 plus per-user ratio scaling. Scout's CommCareBaseLoader._get does bare resp.raise_for_status() with no retry adapter and no Retry-After handling, so a designed throttle response becomes an unhandled HTTPError that fails the source/materialization run. Large-domain syncs (thousands of back-to-back page requests) are exactly the traffic this throttle polices. This RAISES the existing 'Retry/error-shape hardening applied only to Connect loaders' finding from latent to expected-in-production for CommCare; conversely OCS has no app-level throttle upstream, slightly lowering the OCS half.
chain: materialize -> commcare_cases.py:51 self._get(url).json() -> commcare_base.py:61-69 raise_for_status() on 429 -> upstream hqcase/views.py:94 @api_throttle -> api/decorators.py:58 HttpResponse(status=429, Retry-After) -> HTTPError fails the run

votes.evidence:
Scout-side chain fully reproduced and reachable.

commcare_base.py:61-69 `_get`: `resp = self._session.get(...)`; only 401/403 raise CommCareAuthError; then `resp.raise_for_status()` — so a 429 falls through to raise_for_status() and throws requests.HTTPError. No HTTPAdapter/Retry is mounted in __init__ (commcare_base.py:44-47 just creates a bare requests.Session). Confirmed by grep: HTTPAdapter/Retry/mount appear ONLY in connect_base.py (lines 15-16, 61-69, 128-130) where `RETRY_STATUS_FORCELIST = (500,502,503,504,408,429)` and `respect_retry_after_header=True` are mounted. CommCare has none.

Callers (commcare_cases.py:51 `self._get(url, params=params).json()`; commcare_forms.py:51 identical; commcare_metadata.py:53) do not catch HTTPError, so it propagates out of load_pages().

Propagation to run failure: materializer.py:761-765 dispatches CommCareCaseLoader/CommCareFormLoader for source_name in {cases,forms}; _load_source -> writer -> _load_and_commit_source (676-729) catches Exception only to rollback then `raise` (line 724); outer loop at 328-371 records source state='failed' and sets run.state=FAILED (or PARTIAL only if a prior source already committed — cases/forms are non-resumable so no per-page cursor, meaning FAILED unless another source succeeded). Re-raises.

Reachability CONFIRMED: pipelines/commcare_sync.yml declares `provider: commcare` with live `cases` and `forms` sources. materialize_workspace (tasks.py:203-204 @task) and refresh_tenant_schema (125-126) have NO Procrastinate retry= argument; tasks.py:290 comment ('Upstream Connect failure after retry exhaustion') confirms the only retry layer is the Connect in-loader adapter, which CommCare lacks. So a 429 from CommCare Case/Forms API reliably fails the run with no retry at loader, task, or materializer level.

CORRECTION to the finding's framing: it calls this 'Connect-only hardening' and says OCS should be lowered because OCS has no upstream throttle. But ocs_base.py:45-52 `_get` has the SAME bare raise_for_status() pattern with NO retry adapter — so the Scout-side missing-retry gap spans BOTH CommCare and OCS; only Connect is hardened. The OCS half's *Scout-side* exposure is identical to CommCare's regardless of upstream throttle differences.

UNVERIFIABLE here: the upstream CommCare HQ claims (@api_throttle decorator at corehq apps/api/decorators.py:51-61, the specific limits per_second=1/per_minute=10/per_hour=30/per_day=50, HQThrottle via CustomResourceMeta, hqcase/views.py:94, and the 'OCS has no app-level throttle' assertion). None of corehq is in this repo; I could not confirm these. The elevation to 'expected-in-production' and the impact severity rest entirely on these unverified upstream facts.
votes.notes: Verdict PARTIAL: the Scout-side code defect (no 429/Retry-After handling, no retry adapter on CommCare loaders -> HTTPError fails a live, reachable materialization run) is independently CONFIRMED, including the live commcare_sync.yml pipeline and the absence of task-level retry. However the finding's central elevation ('expected-in-production', and the asymmetric raise-CommCare/lower-OCS conclusion) depends on upstream CommCare-HQ throttle facts that are NOT verifiable from this repository — corehq source is not present, so the @api_throttle decorator, the exact rate limits, and the 'OCS has no throttle' claim are unconfirmed here. Additionally the finding mischaracterizes the gap as Connect-only: ocs_base.py:45-52 has the identical bare raise_for_status pattern with no retry adapter, so the Scout-side exposure is CommCare AND OCS, not a reason to lower OCS. Status LATENT is correct (nothing is failing right now absent an actual large-domain CommCare sync hitting the throttle; it is not BROKEN-NOW). Impact correctness is reasonable (a run that should succeed fails); could also be argued cost-perf/velocity.

title: Known finding correction: Case API v2 cannot silently skip under live writes (keyset, dups-at-end by design); skip risk is Forms v0.5 only, narrower than claimed
claim: The reported finding 'CommCare offset pagination under live writes can silently skip records' is REFUTED for the cases loader: Case API v2 is keyset pagination on ('@indexed_on','doc_id') and the docs guarantee a complete oldest-to-newest set where concurrently-updated cases REAPPEAR at the end — duplicates Scout's ON CONFLICT (case_id) absorbs. For Forms v0.5 the claim stands but with corrected mechanism: it is tastypie offset pagination over ES with default ordering '-received_on' DESC (v0_4.py:157), so live inserts shift rows DOWN causing duplicates (absorbed by ON CONFLICT (form_id)), and silent skips occur only when documents leave the result set mid-walk (form archiving/deletion), not from ordinary submission traffic. Severity should be lowered for cases and the forms mechanism re-stated.
chain: commcare_cases.py:38-69 follows v2 next -> upstream get_list.py keyset sort('@indexed_on').sort('doc_id') -> docs: updated cases reappear at end -> materializer L1088 ON CONFLICT (case_id) absorbs; forms: commcare_forms.py:38-71 offset walk -> v0_4.py:157 order_by('-received_on') tastypie limit/offset

votes.evidence:
I independently re-traced both chains from loader entry point through upstream contract to consequence.

CASES (skip claim REFUTED — finding correct):
- mcp_server/loaders/commcare_cases.py:46-68: the loader hits `/a/{domain}/api/case/v2/` with only `{"limit": page_size}` on the first call, then drives pagination purely by following the server URL: line 68 `url = data.get("next")` and line 69 `params = {}`. It never constructs offsets itself.
- Case API v2 docs (commcare-hq.readthedocs.io/api/cases-v2.html, fetched): "this API uses ... 'cursor pagination'"; "you should obtain a complete set of results, ordered from oldest to newest"; "If any cases are updated during the data pull, they may appear again towards the end of the results set." So v2 re-serves updated cases (duplicates), it does not silently skip them under ordinary live writes.
- materializer.py:1088 `ON CONFLICT (case_id) DO UPDATE` absorbs those duplicates. Skip claim correctly does not apply to cases.

FORMS (mechanism re-statement confirmed):
- mcp_server/loaders/commcare_forms.py:44-71: hits `/a/{domain}/api/v0.5/form/` with `{"limit": page_size}`, then follows server pagination via line 70 `url = self._resolve_next_url(initial_url, data.get("meta", {}).get("next"))`, params reset to {}. The offset lives in the server-supplied next URL.
- Upstream corehq/apps/api/resources/v0_4.py (master, fetched verbatim): `XFormInstanceResource.obj_get_list` (~L108-119) ends with `).order_by('-received_on')` (newest-first DESC); the Meta class (~L123-131) sets no paginator_class, so it inherits default tastypie offset/limit pagination. `DoesNothingPaginatorCompat` is on `BaseApplicationResource` (~L196), NOT the forms resource — exactly as the finding asserts when correcting the prior report's v0_4.py:329 note.
- Consequence: under live writes, new submissions insert at offset 0 and shift rows down → offset walk re-serves rows = duplicates, absorbed by materializer.py:1103 `ON CONFLICT (form_id) DO UPDATE`. Silent skips occur only when documents leave the result set mid-walk (archive/delete shifting rows up), not from ordinary submission traffic.

REACHABILITY: materializer.py:57-58 imports both loaders; dispatch at materializer.py:760-765 (`_load_source`) instantiates CommCareCaseLoader/CommCareFormLoader and calls `_write_cases`/`_write_forms`; live entry is `run_commcare_sync` (materializer.py:1967) over the `commcare_sync` pipeline (pipelines/commcare_sync.yml defines sources cases+forms). CommCare sources are excluded from `_RESUMABLE_CONNECT_SOURCES` (materializer.py:772-774), so each run is a full DROP/CREATE/INSERT walk — consistent with single-walk dup absorption. Path is wired and live.
votes.notes: The finding is itself a correction of a prior "CommCare offset pagination can silently skip records" finding; all its substantive claims hold under independent re-trace. One minor line-citation imprecision: the finding cites v0_4.py:157 for `order_by('-received_on')`, but the actual `.order_by('-received_on')` call is in `obj_get_list` (~L119); line 157 region is the Meta `ordering` attribute, which is overridden by the list-query order_by. The DESC `-received_on` ordering on list queries is real and verified, so this does not change the verdict. All upstream evidence is commcare-hq master HEAD; deployed HQ could differ, but the contract (cursor for v2, offset+DESC for forms) is long-standing. Severity is correctly LATENT/correctness: within a single full walk, duplicates are fully absorbed by ON CONFLICT; the only residual data-loss risk (forms skip-on-archive) is genuinely narrow and not triggered by ordinary submission traffic. Cases are unaffected by the skip class entirely.

title: Next-URL trust verified per provider: Case v2 next is settings-derived absolute (safe today); Forms v0.5 relative (handled); Connect next is proxy-header-derived and emitted plaintext http:// in prod (#1109), sending Bearer over plaintext first hop
claim: F7-class verification refining the 'follow next URLs anywhere' finding. (1) Case API v2 'next' is built via corehq reverse(absolute=True) -> get_url_base() = settings.DEFAULT_PROTOCOL + settings.BASE_ADDRESS — always absolute, host from server config; the relative-next trigger is REFUTED for v2, so commcare_cases.py's missing _resolve_next_url is harmless today. (2) Forms v0.5 (tastypie) emits path-relative next links — Scout's _resolve_next_url handles correctly. (3) Connect's IdKeysetPagination.get_next_link uses request.build_absolute_uri — scheme/host from proxy headers; merged PR dimagi/commcare-connect#1109 confirms production emitted http:// next URLs (gunicorn --forwarded-allow-ips default stripped X-Forwarded-Proto), during which Scout's session.get(next) sent the OAuth Bearer over plaintext http for the first hop before following the 301. A Scout-side scheme/host pin would close the recurrence class.
chain: connect_base.py:186-197 session.get(payload['next']) with Authorization header -> upstream pagination.py get_next_link() request.build_absolute_uri -> #1109: gunicorn stripped X-Forwarded-Proto -> http:// next URL -> plaintext request with Bearer, then 301 to https

votes.evidence:
Re-traced independently, all four sub-claims plus reachability:

(1) Case API v2 next is settings-derived absolute — CONFIRMED. commcare-hq corehq/apps/hqcase/views.py:219-220: `res['next'] = reverse('case_api', args=[request.domain], params=res['next'], absolute=True)`; corehq/util/view_utils.py `reverse(... absolute=...)`: `url = "{}{}".format(get_url_base(), url)`; dimagi/utils/web.py:17-22: `get_url_base()` returns `'{}://{}'.format(settings.DEFAULT_PROTOCOL, get_site_domain())` with `get_site_domain()` = `settings.BASE_ADDRESS`. Host/scheme come from server config, not proxy headers. Scout's /Users/bderenzi/Code/dimagi/scout/mcp_server/loaders/commcare_cases.py:68 `url = data.get("next")` indeed never calls `_resolve_next_url`, and that is harmless today exactly as claimed.

(2) Forms v0.5 relative next handled — CONFIRMED. /Users/bderenzi/Code/dimagi/scout/mcp_server/loaders/commcare_forms.py:70 `url = self._resolve_next_url(initial_url, data.get("meta", {}).get("next"))`; commcare_base.py:49-59 `urljoin(base_url, next_url)` resolves absolute, path-relative, and query-only forms.

(3) Connect chain — CONFIRMED mechanically. Upstream: /Users/bderenzi/Code/dimagi/commcare-connect/commcare_connect/data_export/pagination.py:98 `return self.request.build_absolute_uri(f"{self.request.path}?{query.urlencode()}")` — scheme from proxy headers. PR dimagi/commcare-connect#1109 body documents production `connect.dimagi.com` returning `"next": "http://connect.dimagi.com/export/..."` because gunicorn's default `--forwarded-allow-ips=127.0.0.1` strips `X-Forwarded-Proto` from Traefik (SECURE_PROXY_SSL_HEADER in config/settings/production.py:26 is ineffective since the header never reaches Django). Scout side: connect_base.py:126-130 sets `Authorization: Bearer` as a session-level header and mounts adapters on both `https://` and `http://`; lines 186-197 `self._session.get(url, ...)` on `payload["next"]` with no scheme/host pin — a `http://` next URL transmits the full request including the Bearer header in plaintext to port 80 before the edge's 301 arrives; requests then follows the redirect preserving Authorization (same-host http→https upgrade). Scout's own code comment (connect_base.py:187-194) and regression test tests/test_connect_base_loader.py:208-244 (`test_follows_http_to_https_redirect_on_next_url`) pin exactly this behavior and cite #1109.

Reachability — LIVE. apps/workspaces/tasks.py `materialize_workspace` (line 204) imports from mcp_server/services/materializer.py (tasks.py:36), which maps "visits" → ConnectVisitLoader (materializer.py:789) and calls `loader.load_pages(...)` (materializer.py:804/814/838) → `_paginate_export_pages` → the unpinned `session.get(next)`. This runs on every Connect workspace materialization (Procrastinate task).

REFUTED DETAIL — the finding says "merged PR dimagi/commcare-connect#1109". It is NOT merged: `gh pr view 1109` shows state CLOSED, mergedAt null (closed by jjackson 2026-05-07 without merge), and `git grep "forwarded-allow-ips" origin/main` over the whole commcare-connect repo returns nothing — origin/main docker/start is still `gunicorn config.wsgi --bind 0.0.0.0:8000 --chdir=/app --timeout 450 -w 10` with no forwarded flag, and no other gunicorn invocation exists (only docker/start, docker/start_migrate, pyproject.toml, uv.lock mention gunicorn). No in-repo fix landed via any other PR.
votes.notes: The technical chain is fully confirmed — I'd have said CONFIRMED except the finding's key containment fact is wrong, which flips the status. The finding treats #1109 as a merged upstream fix, making the plaintext-Bearer exposure a closed historical window (LATENT recurrence class). In reality #1109 was closed WITHOUT merging and the `--forwarded-allow-ips` fix is absent from commcare-connect main as of today, so unless production was patched out-of-band (not verifiable from here — I could not probe the live `next` field without a Bearer token), every multi-page Connect export Scout runs today still receives `http://` next URLs and sends the OAuth Bearer over plaintext port 80 on the first hop of every page after the first. Scout's connect_base.py comment and regression test treat redirect-following as the mitigation, but that only fixes correctness (pagination continuing), not confidentiality (token already transmitted in cleartext before the 301). The finding's proposed remedy — a Scout-side scheme/host pin on `payload["next"]` (e.g. force-upgrade scheme to https and require host == base_url host before issuing the request) — is therefore not just defense-in-depth against recurrence; it is the only fix Scout controls, and it should be prioritized. Caveat on BROKEN-NOW: contingent on prod running unpatched main; if ops added the gunicorn flag outside the repo, status reverts to LATENT. Recommend Brian confirm with one authenticated request checking the `next` scheme, or just land the pin unconditionally.

votes.evidence:
Independently re-traced all three sub-claims; the technical chain is correct, but one load-bearing fact is wrong (see notes).

(1) Case API v2 absolute-next claim: CONFIRMED. Upstream commcare-hq master, corehq/apps/hqcase/views.py:218-220: `res = get_list(...)` then `if 'next' in res: res['next'] = reverse('case_api', args=[request.domain], params=res['next'], absolute=True)`. corehq/util/view_utils.py:119-129: `if absolute: url = "{}{}".format(get_url_base(), url)`. corehq/ex-submodules/dimagi/utils/web.py:17-22: `get_url_base()` returns `'{}://{}'.format(settings.DEFAULT_PROTOCOL, settings.BASE_ADDRESS)` — scheme/host from server settings, not request headers; get_list.py:115-116 builds `next` as a cursor dict that views.py converts to this absolute URL. So Scout's /Users/bderenzi/Code/dimagi/scout/mcp_server/loaders/commcare_cases.py:68 (`url = data.get("next")` with no `_resolve_next_url`) is indeed harmless today for v2.

(2) Forms v0.5 relative-next handling: CONFIRMED. /Users/bderenzi/Code/dimagi/scout/mcp_server/loaders/commcare_forms.py:70 calls `self._resolve_next_url(initial_url, data.get("meta", {}).get("next"))`; commcare_base.py:49-59 urljoin-resolves path-relative/query-only tastypie next links.

(3) Connect plaintext-Bearer chain: CONFIRMED mechanically. Local checkout /Users/bderenzi/Code/dimagi/commcare-connect/commcare_connect/data_export/pagination.py:88-98: `get_next_link()` returns `self.request.build_absolute_uri(f"{self.request.path}?{query.urlencode()}")` — scheme from proxy headers. Scout /Users/bderenzi/Code/dimagi/scout/mcp_server/loaders/connect_base.py:126-130 sets session-level `Authorization: Bearer` and mounts the retry adapter on both `https://` AND `http://`; lines 186-197 `self._session.get(url, ...)` follows `payload.get("next")` (line 234) verbatim with `allow_redirects=True`, so an `http://` next URL gets the Bearer on the plaintext first hop before the 301. Scout's own regression test tests/test_connect_base_loader.py:208-244 (`test_follows_http_to_https_redirect_on_next_url`) pins exactly this http-next → 301 → https flow with auth preserved. Reachability: live — connect loaders are consumed by mcp_server/services/materializer.py, imported by apps/workspaces/tasks.py:36 (procrastinate materialization tasks).

(4) The "#1109" fact is WRONG in one critical way: `gh pr view 1109` in dimagi/commcare-connect shows state=CLOSED, mergedAt=null (created 2026-04-08, closed unmerged 2026-05-07). `git show origin/main:docker/start` (after fresh fetch) still reads `gunicorn config.wsgi --bind 0.0.0.0:8000 --chdir=/app --timeout 450 -w 10` — no `--forwarded-allow-ips`. No other PR matching "forwarded-allow-ips" exists. config/settings/production.py:26 sets SECURE_PROXY_SSL_HEADER, but per the PR body gunicorn strips X-Forwarded-Proto before Django sees it. Deploy uses a server-side env-file (deploy/config/deploy.production.yml: /home/connect/www/commcare-connect/docker.env) that is not in-repo, so an out-of-band FORWARDED_ALLOW_IPS env fix cannot be ruled out — but nothing in the repo fixes it.
votes.notes: The finding describes #1109 as a "merged PR" and frames the plaintext-Bearer exposure as a past window ("during which... before following the 301"). Both are misstated: #1109 was closed WITHOUT merging, the gunicorn flag is absent from origin/main's docker/start, and no successor PR exists. Unless ops independently set FORWARDED_ALLOW_IPS in the server-side docker.env (unverifiable from the repo), production Connect still emits http:// next URLs today — Scout's own freshly-added comment (connect_base.py:187-194, 'has been observed') and regression test treat it as current behavior and deliberately codify following the plaintext hop. That makes this an ongoing per-materialization exposure (every paginated Connect export page after the first sends the Bearer token in cleartext to port 80 of connect.dimagi.com until the edge 301s), not a latent recurrence class. Corrections: status LATENT → BROKEN-NOW (conditional only on the unknown server-side env override); the recommended Scout-side https scheme pin (upgrade http→https on next URLs for the known host before requesting) becomes an active mitigation, not just hardening. The rest of the finding (v2 refutation of relative-next for cases, forms v0.5 handled) is accurate as written.

title: Connect v2 export responses never contain 'count': progress totals always None and loader docstrings describe a nonexistent field
claim: Verified verbatim: IdKeysetPagination.get_paginated_response returns only {'next', 'results'} — no count, ever. Scout's connect_base.py reads payload.get('count') and documents 'total_count is the count field from the first response' (connect_base.py:165-167, 225-229); every Connect loader threads page_total to the materializer's rows_total. All Connect materialization progress is therefore permanently indeterminate (honest, but undocumented as such), and the docstring is a comment-as-claim failure. Contrast: OCS upstream added a first-page 'count' explicitly citing Scout as the consumer; nobody requested the same from Connect.
chain: upstream IdKeysetPagination.get_paginated_response returns {'next','results'} only -> connect_base.py:165-167 reads payload.get('count') (always None) -> page_total threaded to rows_total -> indeterminate progress; docstring claims a count field that never exists

votes.evidence:
Core mechanism CONFIRMED by my own re-trace:

1. mcp_server/loaders/connect_base.py:225-229 reads the never-sent field verbatim:
   `if first_page: total = payload.get("count"); if not isinstance(total, int): total = None; yield payload["results"], total`. So Connect's page envelope total is always None on every code path (upstream get_paginated_response returns only {'next','results'} per the verbatim-curl report at docs/arch-review/2026-06-12/reports/gap1-upstream-provider-contract-verifier.md:68-71; I could not curl upstream from this sandbox but the prior report's verbatim quote is consistent).

2. The docstring IS a comment-as-claim failure: connect_base.py:165-167 — "``total_count`` is the ``count`` field from the first response only" — asserts a field that the v2 paginator never emits. Confirmed verbatim.

3. page_total IS threaded to rows_total: every Connect loader yields (page, page_total) from _paginate_export_pages (connect_visits.py:76-83, connect_invoices.py:25-31, connect_users.py:23-27, connect_payments.py, connect_completed_works.py, connect_completed_modules.py, connect_assessments.py). The materializer writers consume it: materializer.py:867-872 `rows_total = page_total` then on_page(total, rows_total) -> make_on_page._on_page (materializer.py:154-176) -> progress_updater dict key "rows_total". Reachable: connect_sync.yml is a live pipeline with 7 sources, loaded by pipeline_registry from /pipelines.

WHERE THE FINDING IS WRONG (severity/scope overstated): The claim "All Connect materialization progress is therefore permanently indeterminate" is FALSE for the primary visits source on fresh loads. make_on_page has a `known_total` fallback (materializer.py:160 `effective_total = rows_total if rows_total is not None else known_total`). For the visits source, materializer.py:291-293 passes `known_total=source_total` where `source_total = visit_total if source.name == "visits" and start_cursor is None`. visit_total is derived from the opportunity's `visit_count` discovered up front via /export/opp_org_program_list/ during the DISCOVER phase (materializer.py:208-212, _connect_visit_total at materializer.py:547-561). So a fresh Connect visits load DOES show a real progress percent independent of the missing `count`. Only (a) visits-on-resume and (b) the other 6 id-less Connect sources are genuinely indeterminate. The finding (and the gap1 report it derives from, 1.3) both missed this fallback.
votes.notes: Net: the docstring defect (comment-as-claim, COSMETIC/velocity) is real and confirmed — connect_base.py:165-167 documents a `count` field the upstream v2 paginator never sends, and that dead read threads through to rows_total. But the consequence framing ("ALL Connect progress permanently indeterminate") is incorrect: the visits source (the dominant Connect dataset by volume) gets an honest, real-percent denominator on fresh loads from the discovered visit_count fallback, with no dependence on the missing field. So the accurate scope is: indeterminate for the 6 id-less sources + visits-on-resume; determinate for visits-on-fresh-load. Status COSMETIC and impact velocity are appropriate for the docstring defect itself. Recommend re-titling to "Connect page-envelope `count` never sent — loader docstring claims a nonexistent field; affects progress totals for all Connect sources EXCEPT visits-on-fresh-load (which falls back to discovered visit_count)." Verified-by-trace on the Scout side; upstream contract relied on the prior reviewer's verbatim curl, not independently re-fetched (sandbox has no network to raw.githubusercontent.com).

title: raw_visits.images is always empty: upstream only emits images with ?images=true, which Scout never sends, yet the writer creates and upserts an images JSONB column
claim: Verified verbatim: UserVisitDataView selects UserVisitDataWithImagesSerializer only when request has images=true; the default serializer omits the 'images' key entirely. ConnectVisitLoader passes no params, so raw.get('images') or [] is always []. Yet the writer creates an images JSONB column and upserts it (images=EXCLUDED.images, materializer ~L1273/L1445/L1483) — an advertised, perpetually-empty column visible to the agent and analysts. Either request images=true (payload-size tradeoff) or drop the column.
chain: connect_visits.py:76-78 _paginate_export_pages('user_visits/') no params -> upstream UserVisitDataView.get_serializer_class: images only when query_params['images']=='true' -> connect_visits.py:39-41,65 defaults to [] -> materializer ~L1445 images JSONB always []

votes.evidence:
I independently re-traced the full chain from entry point to consequence, reading the actual files (not the finding's quotes), and even verified the upstream provider contract against the real CommCare Connect source checked out at /Users/bderenzi/Code/dimagi/commcare-connect.

1) Loader sends NO images param. mcp_server/loaders/connect_visits.py:76-78 — `for page, page_total in self._paginate_export_pages("user_visits/", start_last_id=start_last_id)` passes no `params`. mcp_server/loaders/connect_base.py:147 — `_paginate_export_pages(self, suffix, params=None, start_last_id=None)`; line 179 `request_params = dict(params) if params else None`; only `last_id` is ever injected (L180-182). No `images` key is ever sent. Confirmed via grep: the only "images" references in mcp_server/loaders/*.py are the defaulting logic and the output key.

2) Upstream contract (verified against real source). commcare-connect/commcare_connect/data_export/views.py:260-266 — `UserVisitDataView._include_images()` returns `self.request.query_params.get("images", "").lower() == "true"`; `get_serializer_class` returns `UserVisitDataWithImagesSerializer` only when that is true, else `UserVisitDataSerializer`. The v2 paginated-JSON path (which Scout uses via Accept header `application/json; version=2.0`, connect_base.py:29) is views.py:137-145: `if self.request.version == "2.0": ... serializer_class = self.get_serializer_class()` (L142) then `serializer = serializer_class(page, many=True)` (L143). serializer.py:95-122 — default `UserVisitDataSerializer.Meta.fields` has NO `"images"`; only `UserVisitDataWithImagesSerializer` (L128-132) appends `+ ["images"]`. So with no `?images=true`, the JSON `results` rows omit the `images` key entirely.

3) Loader defaults to empty. connect_visits.py:39 — `images = raw.get("images") or []` → always `[]` since the key is absent.

4) Writer creates + upserts the perpetually-empty column. mcp_server/services/materializer.py:1445 `images JSONB` in CREATE TABLE raw_visits; L1483 `json.dumps(r.get("images") or [])` in the INSERT tuple; L1273 `images=EXCLUDED.images` in the ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE.

5) Reachability confirmed (live, not dead code). pipelines/connect_sync.yml:4 `provider: commcare_connect`, line 7 `- name: visits` — an active declared source. Dispatch: materializer.py:743 `_load_connect_source` -> L789 `"visits": (ConnectVisitLoader, _write_connect_visits)`; "visits" is in `_RESUMABLE_CONNECT_SOURCES` (L772-773). The raw_visits.images column is part of the materialized schema for every Connect tenant.

The finding's quoted line numbers (connect_visits.py:39-41,65; materializer.py:1445/1273/1483) are all accurate.
votes.notes: Severity/status calibration is correct: DEBT / correctness. It is not a crash or data-loss — the column simply always materializes as JSONB `[]`, an advertised-but-empty column that an analyst or the agent could reasonably assume holds image references. The fix options stated (send `images=true`, accepting the payload-size/prefetch tradeoff visible at views.py:275-297 `_prefetch_images`, or drop the column) are accurate. Minor nuance for the remediation owner: upstream `get_images` returns `[{blob_id, name, parent_id}]` metadata only (serializer.py:134-138), not the image bytes, so the payload cost of opting in is modest — that strengthens the "either request it or drop it" framing. No refutation found; chain reproduced end to end.

title: raw_sessions.participant_platform always '' (OCS nested participant serializer has no platform field); sessions->participants join on bare identifier conflates same-identifier participants across platforms
claim: Verified verbatim: ParticipantSerializer (nested in ExperimentSessionSerializer) has fields=['identifier','remote_id'] only. Scout's _map_session reads participant.get('platform') — a key the provider never sends — so participant_platform is empty for every row, while the session-level 'platform' field that does exist is ignored. Additionally, OCS participants are unique per (team, platform, identifier) (get_or_create in ExperimentSessionCreateSerializer), so the ocs_sync.yml sessions->participants join on bare identifier can conflate same-identifier participants across platforms.
chain: ocs_sessions.py:39-57 _map_session participant.get('platform') -> upstream ParticipantSerializer fields=['identifier','remote_id'] -> participant_platform always '' in raw_sessions; ocs_sync.yml join on bare identifier conflates across platforms

votes.evidence:
I re-traced the full chain and independently verified both sub-claims, including against the real upstream OCS source (not just Scout's mock tests).

SCOUT SIDE (this repo):
- mcp_server/loaders/ocs_sessions.py:39-57 — _map_session reads `participant = raw.get("participant") or {}` then sets `"participant_platform": participant.get("platform") or ""` (line 53). The nested session-participant object is the only source it consults; the session-level platform field is never read.
- mcp_server/services/materializer.py:904-908 creates raw_sessions with a `participant_platform TEXT` column, and lines 925-936 insert `r.get("participant_platform", "")` per row — so the dead value lands in the materialized table.
- pipelines/ocs_sync.yml — join is at lines 33-37 (finding cited 38-42, off by ~5; minor): `from_table: raw_sessions / from_column: participant_identifier -> to_table: raw_participants / to_column: identifier`.
- materializer.py:1021-1031 — raw_participants PK is `participant_id` (the OCS participant UUID), and `identifier` is a plain non-unique TEXT column. So a join on bare `identifier` is many-to-many across platform variants, exactly as the finding states.

UPSTREAM CONTRACT (verified against raw.githubusercontent.com/dimagi/open-chat-studio/main):
- apps/api/serializers.py: `ParticipantSerializer.Meta.fields = ["identifier", "remote_id"]` (NO platform), and `ExperimentSessionSerializer.participant = ParticipantSerializer(read_only=True)`. The session serializer itself DOES carry a `platform` field that Scout ignores. => `participant.get("platform")` is always None -> "" for every raw_sessions row.
- apps/experiments/models.py: `Participant` has `platform = models.CharField(max_length=32)` and `unique_together = [("team", "platform", "identifier")]`.
- apps/api/serializers.py ExperimentSessionCreateSerializer: `Participant.objects.get_or_create(identifier=..., team=request.team, platform=ChannelPlatform.API, defaults={"user": request.user})` — identity key is (team, platform, identifier), confirming bare-identifier joins can conflate.

REACHABILITY (live today):
- mcp_server/pipeline_registry.py:84-97 auto-loads every pipelines/*.yml at runtime and resolves by provider (get_by_provider, lines 102-112), so ocs_sync.yml is live for any OCS tenant.
- mcp_server/services/metadata.py:348-359 emits the relationships list (the bare-identifier join) into the metadata snapshot.
- mcp_server/server.py:231-286 — `get_metadata` is a registered `@mcp.tool()` that returns `relationships` to the agent. So the misleading join hint and the always-empty participant_platform column are both surfaced to a live agent tool.
votes.notes: Both sub-claims are factually correct and I confirmed them against the live upstream OCS source, so this is CONFIRMED rather than PARTIAL. DEBT/correctness is the right classification: nothing crashes and the materialization succeeds, but (1) participant_platform is a silently-dead/always-empty column in raw_sessions (the session-level platform that DOES exist is dropped), and (2) the relationship hint joins on a non-unique identifier so the agent may conflate same-identifier participants across platforms when it follows the declared FK. Severity is correctly modest: it degrades analytical correctness/agent reasoning, not data integrity of writes. Minor inaccuracy in the finding: the yml join lines are 33-37, not 38-42 (the loader cite ocs_sessions.py:52-53 is exact). The fix is two small changes: read the session-level `platform` in _map_session, and either widen the join to (identifier, platform) or join raw_messages/sessions to raw_participants via the participant UUID once it's threaded through. Reviewer tag 'gap1-upstream-provider-contract-verifier' is apt — the bug originates entirely from Scout assuming a nested-participant field shape the provider contract never promised; Scout's own tests (tests/test_ocs_data_loaders.py:50,87) encode the wrong assumption via mocks, which is why it went unnoticed.
